revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:郡司]][[ru:Гундзи]][[uk:Ґундзі]][[zh:郡司]]
'''Gunjishi''' (郡司, also read as "Kōri no Tsukasa") refers to local officials in [[Ritsuryō system|Ritsuryō Japan]] who governed each {{ill|Gun (administrative division)|en|Gun (administrative division)|qid=Q20113851|lt=Gun|1=Gun (administrative division)}} (郡, district) under the jurisdiction of a provincial governor ({{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|qid=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}}). These positions were held by influential local figures who were often appointed hereditarily.
== Overview == During the establishment of the [[Ritsuryō system]] following the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]], the traditional governance of local aristocrats ([[Gōzoku]]) was abolished, and their ruling rights were subsumed by the central state. These figures were reappointed as local administrative officials (Gunjishi) based on the clan system of ancient Japan. They were responsible for governance, including maintaining household registries and managing tax collection.
In approximately the 5th year of {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|qid=Q1077930|lt=Taika (era)|1=Taika (era)}} (649 CE), the "Kuni" (国) governed by local aristocrats such as the [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] was abolished, and administrative units called [[:en:Kōri|Kōri]] (評) were established. Local aristocrats were appointed as [[Hiyōzu]] or [[Hiyōtoku]] to manage these units.
With the enactment of the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} in 701 CE, the "Kōri" was replaced with "Gun" (郡), and Gunjishi positions were organized into a [[Four-tier system]]: * '''Dairyō''' (大領, chief official), * '''Shōryō''' (少領, deputy official), * '''Shusei''' (主政, administrative assistant), * '''Shuchō''' (主帳, record keeper). Dairyō and Shōryō, with their significant authority, were collectively referred to as "Gunryō."
Unlike centrally dispatched officials like {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|qid=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}}, Gunjishi were appointed from local aristocrats, such as former [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], in a hereditary and lifelong capacity. Moreover, Gunjishi were excluded from the [[Kan'i Rank system]] outlined in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}, emphasizing their unique status within the Ritsuryō framework.
== Role in Ritsuryō Governance == Gunjishi wielded substantial authority, managing tax collection, storage, transportation, and land redistribution under the [[Handen-Shūju system]]. Early Ritsuryō governance thus operated through a dual structure where central authority via Kokushi was mediated by the local power of Gunjishi.
The {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|qid=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} gradually reorganized administrative units by splitting larger Gun or incorporating additional [[:en:Sato|Sato]] (village units). This effort weakened the direct influence of Gunjishi and limited monopolization of these positions by specific clans. In cases where multiple aristocratic families resided within a Gun, appointments were rotated to ensure fairness.
== Decline of Gunjishi == As the Ritsuryō system weakened during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Gunjishi began to lose their administrative roles. Tax collection and the management of state granaries were transferred to the Kokushi. The diminishing roles of Gunjishi resulted in their transformation into [[Zaichō Kanjin]], local officials serving under Kokushi, or into landowners managing [[Shōen]] estates.
By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, many descendants of Gunjishi transitioned into the [[Bushidan]], local warrior clans, participating in the feudal military order.
== Notable Gunjishi == * [[Musashi Takeshiba]] - A Gunjishi involved in the [[Jōhei-Tengyō War]]. * [[Fujiwara no Motonaga]] - Criticized in the [[Owari Province Appeal]] for governance failures.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|qid=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} - Provincial governor. * {{ill|Gun|en|Gun|qid=Q110155210|lt=Gun|1=Gun}} - Administrative district. * [[Handen-Shūju system]] - Land allocation system under the Ritsuryō. * [[Shōen]] - Private estates. * [[Zaichō Kanjin]] - Local bureaucrats under Kokushi.
== References == * ''Shinseiban Nihonshi Jiten'', [[Suken Publishing]]. * ''Obunsha Nihonshi Jiten'', [[Obunsha]].
{{translated page|ja|郡司|version=103423508|comment=Imported full ja history}}
